name: NavDeepLinks
tagline: Open a planet detail screen straight from an outside URI like navdemo://planet/3, with a sensible Back stack.
whatItDoes: Launched normally, the app shows a Categories list (Rocky Planets, Gas Giants); tapping a category opens its planet list, and tapping a planet opens that planet's detail screen with a Back button. But the real point is deep linking: an outside source (a browser, a notification, or an adb command) can fire a URI such as navdemo://planet/3 to jump the user DIRECTLY onto Earth's detail screen with no taps. Crucially, pressing Back from that deep-linked screen does not exit the app; it walks up Detail to Items to Categories, exactly as if the user had navigated there by hand. This works because the app hand-seeds a three-entry back stack when it handles a deep link.
mentalModel: A deep link should not just show one screen; it should SEED a believable multi-entry back stack so that pressing Back walks the user 'up' through the app instead of dumping them out.

screens.name: CategoriesScreen
screens.role: Level 1: a LazyColumn of planet categories (Rocky / Gas Giants); tapping a row pushes an ItemsKey for that category.

screens.name: ItemsScreen
screens.role: Level 2: a header naming the chosen category plus a scrolling list of its planets, with a 'Back to categories' button.

screens.name: DetailScreen
screens.role: Level 3: shows one planet's title and blurb with a Back button; this is the screen a deep link lands on.

screens.name: AppNavigation
screens.role: Hosts the NavBackStack and entryProvider, seeded from caller-supplied initialKeys so a deep link can start with 3 entries instead of 1.

concepts.plain: A URI fired from OUTSIDE the app (browser, notification, adb) that jumps straight to a specific screen. Here navdemo://planet/3 opens Earth's detail screen directly, no tapping required.
The AndroidManifest block declaring scheme navdemo and host planet plus action VIEW and categories DEFAULT + BROWSABLE. This is what tells Android to route navdemo://planet/... URIs to MainActivity.
buildInitialBackStack returns a hand-built list [CategoriesKey, ItemsKey(category), DetailKey(itemId)] for a valid deep link, so Back has somewhere to go instead of exiting the app.
A COLD start reads the URI from intent.data in onCreate; a WARM start (app already alive) arrives via onNewIntent. The activity handles both and stores the URI in deepLinkUri.

AppNavigation builds the stack inside remember(initialKeys). When a warm-start deep link changes the URI, initialKeys changes, the remembered stack is thrown away, and the navigation re-seeds to the new screen.

parseItemId uses lastPathSegment and toIntOrNull, and categoryIdForItem uses firstOrNull, so an untrusted URI like navdemo://planet/abc or planet/99 yields null and falls back to Categories instead of crashing.
Each screen is a @Serializable NavKey (CategoriesKey, ItemsKey, DetailKey); entryProvider's entry<KeyType> blocks map the top key to the right composable, and NavDisplay renders it.
Keys carry just an Int id (categoryId / itemId), not whole objects; screens resolve the full Category/Item via categoryById / itemById, keeping each key tiny and serializable.

files.role: The entire app: data, nav keys, deep-link parsing, the Activity that reads the intent, and all the Compose screens.
Declares MainActivity with two intent-filters: the normal LAUNCHER one and the deep-link VIEW one (scheme navdemo, host planet).

highlights.title: The deep-link intent-filter (scheme + host)
highlights.explain: This second intent-filter is what makes deep linking possible. action VIEW lets browsers/adb route URIs here; category DEFAULT is required to match an implicit intent and BROWSABLE allows opening from a web context; the data element with scheme 'navdemo' and host 'planet' declares which URIs match (navdemo://planet/...). The trailing /id is left for code to parse. Without this block the URI would never reach the app.

highlights.title: Turning a URI into a seeded back stack
highlights.explain: buildInitialBackStack is the core idea of the sample. For a valid deep link it returns a three-entry list [CategoriesKey, ItemsKey(categoryId), DetailKey(itemId)] ordered bottom-to-top, so Back can walk up the app. For a missing or bogus link it returns just [CategoriesKey]. It is a pure function (URI in, List<NavKey> out) with no Compose or Activity state, which makes the 'URI to stack' logic easy to reason about and test.

highlights.title: Safe parsing of an untrusted URI
highlights.explain: parseItemId pulls the last path segment ('3' from navdemo://planet/3) and converts it with toIntOrNull, NOT toInt. Because the URI is external, untrusted input, a non-numeric segment like 'abc' must yield null rather than throw. Returning null simply means 'no valid deep link', which causes a graceful fallback to the Categories screen.

highlights.title: Cold vs warm start: reading the deep link
highlights.explain: deepLinkUri is mutableStateOf so the UI reacts when it changes. onCreate (a COLD start) reads intent.data once. onNewIntent (a WARM start, when the activity is already alive) is essential because onCreate will not run again; it calls setIntent so getIntent reflects the new URI, then updates deepLinkUri, triggering a recompose and re-seed.

highlights.title: Seeding and re-seeding the NavBackStack
highlights.explain: remember(initialKeys) builds a NavBackStack from the caller's keys by spreading the list (*initialKeys.toTypedArray()). Passing initialKeys as the remember key is the trick: when a warm-start deep link changes the URI, initialKeys changes, the old stack is discarded, and a fresh seeded stack is built. NavDisplay renders the top key and onBack pops with removeLastOrNull, so a deep-linked user walks Detail to Items to Categories.

highlights.title: The three nav keys as @Serializable NavKeys
highlights.explain: CategoriesKey is a data object (a singleton, since there is only one categories screen and it carries no arguments), while ItemsKey(categoryId) and DetailKey(itemId) are data classes that each carry a single Int id. All implement NavKey and are @Serializable so the back stack survives process death. DetailKey is the key a deep link ultimately lands on.

newVsPrev: NavFourScreen taught the basic Nav3 setup: a single NavBackStack, NavDisplay, entryProvider, and tapping forward/back through several screens. NavDeepLinks adds entry FROM OUTSIDE the app. It introduces a second AndroidManifest intent-filter (action VIEW, scheme navdemo, host planet), reads the launch URI via intent.data and warm-start URIs via onNewIntent, parses that URI defensively (parseItemId with toIntOrNull), and most importantly hand-seeds a MULTI-entry back stack (buildInitialBackStack) so Back works naturally instead of exiting. The initial keys now come from the caller (initialKeys) and re-seed via remember(initialKeys), which NavFourScreen never needed.
tryThis: Run the deep-link adb command with different ids (navdemo://planet/1 through /6) and watch which planet opens. Then try navdemo://planet/99 and navdemo://planet/abc and confirm the app falls back to the Categories screen instead of crashing.

After a valid deep link, press Back repeatedly and observe the path Detail to Items to Categories to exit. Then temporarily change buildInitialBackStack to return listOf(DetailKey(itemId)) only and re-test: now Back exits the app immediately, demonstrating exactly why the synthetic stack matters.

Add a Log.d line or a breakpoint in onNewIntent, launch the app normally, send it to the background, fire the adb command, and confirm the warm-start path runs (not onCreate) yet still navigates to the new planet.

Change the manifest data element host from 'planet' to 'world' and rebuild; the old navdemo://planet/3 URI will stop matching (no activity found), showing how scheme and host gate which URIs reach the app.
Remove category DEFAULT from the deep-link intent-filter, rebuild, and try the adb command; the implicit VIEW intent will no longer match, proving DEFAULT is required for implicit deep links.
gotchas: The 'navdemo' scheme is invented just for this demo so it owns the URI outright; a real production app would normally use https:// App Links (with domain verification) rather than a custom scheme.

Deep-link parsing here is deliberately by-hand (intent.data, onNewIntent, lastPathSegment) and avoids Nav3 alpha-only deep-link helper APIs, per the assignment, so it sticks to the stable Nav3 1.0.x surface.

The sample only seeds the back stack from the URI; it does not, for example, validate that the seeded ItemsKey category actually contains the item beyond looking it up. The data is a tiny hardcoded in-memory list, not a database or network source.

categoryById and itemById use first { } which would throw if given an unknown id, but the deep-link path only ever uses categoryIdForItem (firstOrNull) on untrusted input, so by the time a key is built the id is already known-good.
runTest: Build and install on an emulator or device with ./gradlew installDebug (or Run in Android Studio). Open it from the launcher to confirm the normal Categories flow. Then deep-link from a terminal with: adb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW -d 'navdemo://planet/4' com.example.navdeeplinks (planet/4 = Mars). The app should open DIRECTLY on Mars's detail screen, and pressing Back should walk Mars to the Rocky Planets list to Categories rather than exiting. Try /99 or /abc to see the graceful fallback to Categories. Filter Logcat by 'DeepLink' to watch parseItemId and buildInitialBackStack log what they seeded.
